title: Meta Will Record Employees' Keystrokes and Screens to Train Workplace AI
subtitle: Internal program dubbed 'Model Capability Initiative' installs tracking software on US employees' computers — raising labor, privacy, and legal questions.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Meta has begun installing monitoring software on US-based employees' work computers that captures mouse movements, clicks, keystrokes, and periodic screenshots, and feeding that data into training for its internal AI agent models, according to reporting published April 21. The program is called the Model Capability Initiative (MCI) and an internal memo frames it as a way to improve the company's workplace agents in areas where they currently struggle — navigating dropdown menus, handling multi-window workflows, and using keyboard shortcuts the way an experienced user would. The rollout is focused initially on employees in technical and product roles.

The mechanism matters: large language models trained on internet text know what computer use looks like described in prose, but not what it looks like as a stream of user actions. Capturing real employee behavior generates the same kind of demonstration data that recent 'computer use' agent models from Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google have been trained on, but from a captive and internally labeled source. For Meta, that solves a data-access bottleneck. For the employees being recorded, it collapses the distinction between doing work and producing training data — a degree of monitoring that has, until now, been mostly limited to warehouse and delivery workers.

The program also runs into patchy legal ground. Italy explicitly prohibits electronic monitoring used to measure employee productivity. German courts have held that keystroke logging is permissible only in narrow cases, such as investigations into serious misconduct. US labor law is more permissive, but several states require notice and consent, and unions have begun flagging the practice as a collective-bargaining matter. This is the first time a hyperscaler has publicly confirmed using its own workforce as a training corpus, and it will likely set a reference point — positive or negative — for how the rest of the industry sources demonstration data for agent models.

For learners, the useful frame is that training-data provenance is becoming the central policy question of AI, not model size. The capabilities of agentic systems increasingly depend on who was recorded, under what consent, and for which tasks. When evaluating AI tools at work or school, it is now reasonable to ask what the training data was, whether the producers were informed, and what recourse they have. Those questions were fringe three years ago; they are now the main event.

sources.title: Meta will record employees' keystrokes and use it to train its AI models — TechCrunch
Meta will start tracking employees' screens and keystrokes to train AI tools — Fortune
Meta to start capturing employee mouse movements, keystrokes for AI training data — Reuters via Seeking Alpha
Meta Plans to Turn Its Employees' Clicks and Keystrokes into AI Training Data — Gizmodo
